title: Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil

seo.title: Authentic Focaccia Genovese Recipe | Rosemary & Olive Oil
seo.metaDescription: Master authentic Focaccia Genovese with our easy recipe. Learn to bake this traditional Italian bread infused with rosemary and olive oil. Perfect for any meal!
longTailKeywords: Focaccia Genovese with rosemary and olive oil
seo.h1: Authentic Focaccia Genovese with Rosemary and Olive Oil
seo.imageAlt: Golden brown Focaccia Genovese with fresh rosemary and olive oil, a classic Italian bread.
seo.imageCaption: Our homemade Focaccia Genovese, baked to perfection with fragrant rosemary and rich olive oil.

description: Imagine the aroma of freshly baked bread filling your kitchen, a golden Focaccia Genovese emerging from the oven. This classic Italian flatbread, kissed with fragrant rosemary and rich olive oil, offers a delightfully crispy crust and a tender, airy interior. Perfect as an appetizer, a side to a main course, or simply enjoyed on its own.

ingredients.notes: plus more for drizzling and pan

instructions.title: Activate the Yeast
instructions.text: In a large bowl, combine the warm water and yeast, along with a pinch of sugar. Stir gently and let it sit for 5-10 minutes until the mixture becomes foamy, indicating the yeast is active.

instructions.title: Prepare the Dough
instructions.text: Add the strong bread flour and fine sea salt to the yeast mixture. Mix with a sturdy spoon or your hands until a shaggy, cohesive dough forms. Drizzle in 2 tablespoons of extra virgin olive oil and continue to mix until it is well incorporated.

instructions.title: Knead the Dough
instructions.text: Transfer the dough to a lightly floured surface. Knead for 8-10 minutes until the dough is smooth, elastic, and no longer sticky. If using a stand mixer, use the dough hook attachment and knead for 5-7 minutes on medium-low speed.

instructions.title: First Rise
instructions.text: Lightly oil a clean bowl. Place the kneaded dough in the bowl, turning it once to coat with oil. Cover the bowl with plastic wrap or a damp kitchen towel and let it rise in a warm place for 1.5 to 2 hours, or until the dough has doubled in size.

instructions.title: Shape and Second Rise
instructions.text: Generously oil a 9x13 inch baking pan with extra virgin olive oil. Gently transfer the risen dough into the pan and, using your fingertips, gently press and dimple the dough all over. Drizzle generously with more olive oil, scatter the fresh rosemary leaves, and sprinkle with coarse sea salt. Cover and let rise for another 30-45 minutes.

instructions.title: Bake the Focaccia
instructions.text: Preheat your oven to 400F (200C). Bake the focaccia for 25-35 minutes, or until the top is golden brown and the bread is cooked through. For extra richness, you can drizzle a little more olive oil over the top immediately after removing it from the oven.

instructions.title: Cool and Serve
instructions.text: Carefully remove the focaccia from the baking pan and transfer it to a wire rack to cool slightly. Slice and serve warm. This focaccia is best enjoyed fresh on the day it is baked.

tips: For an extra airy interior, ensure your dough is well-hydrated. Do not be afraid if the dough feels sticky; resist adding too much extra flour during kneading to maintain its tender texture.

To achieve a more authentic Genovese texture, create small indentations (dimples) in the dough before the second rise. You can even fill these dimples with a brine made from water and olive oil for a classic savory touch.
Focaccia is best enjoyed warm and fresh from the oven. If you have leftovers, store them wrapped tightly at room temperature for up to two days, or freeze slices for longer storage and reheat gently.
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to the sun-drenched Ligurian coast of Italy, where a simple yet profound bread reigns supreme: Focaccia Genovese. This isn't just any focaccia; it's a masterpiece of texture and flavor, characterized by its impossibly airy interior, shatteringly crisp crust, and the intoxicating aroma of rosemary and high-quality olive oil. Far from being a mere accompaniment, the traditional Focaccia Genovese is a beloved staple, enjoyed throughout the day, from breakfast to dinner. Its preparation is an art form that celebrates minimal ingredients and masterful technique, resulting in a bread that captures the very essence of Italian baking. If you've ever dreamt of recreating that authentic taste of Italy in your own kitchen, perfecting this iconic bread is an excellent place to start.

## What is Focaccia Genovese?
At its heart, Focaccia Genovese is a flatbread from Genoa, the capital of Italy’s Liguria region. Unlike its thicker, often more bread-like counterparts found in other parts of Italy, the Genovese version is distinctly thin, almost pizza-like in its base, yet incredibly soft and porous within. Its signature characteristic is a dimpled surface, created by pressing fingers into the dough before baking, which traps puddles of olive oil and creates pockets for steam, contributing to its unique texture. The generous application of extra virgin olive oil, both within the dough and on its surface, is non-negotiable, infusing every bite with a rich, fruity flavor. Topped simply with fresh rosemary and coarse sea salt, it embodies the Ligurian philosophy of letting high-quality ingredients shine. This is not just bread; it's a vehicle for olive oil, a testament to the region's culinary heritage, and a dish that truly stands on its own.

## Ingredients for Focaccia Genovese
The beauty of Focaccia Genovese lies in its humble list of ingredients, each playing a crucial role in achieving that perfect balance of flavor and texture.
*   **Strong Bread Flour:** A high-protein flour is essential for developing the gluten structure needed to create the signature airy crumb and chew.
*   **Water:** Lukewarm water helps activate the yeast and hydrate the flour. The exact amount can vary slightly depending on your flour, but the dough should be quite wet.
*   **Fresh Yeast or Active Dry Yeast:** Yeast is the leavening agent, giving the focaccia its rise and characteristic texture. Fresh yeast offers a slightly superior flavor, but active dry works perfectly well.
*   **Fine Sea Salt (for dough):** Essential for flavor and controlling yeast activity.
*   **Sugar (optional):** A pinch of sugar can help "wake up" the yeast, especially if you're using active dry yeast.
*   **High-Quality Extra Virgin Olive Oil:** This is perhaps the most critical ingredient. A fruity, robust extra virgin olive oil from Liguria or a similar region will impart the authentic flavor. It's used both in the dough and generously drizzled on top.
*   **Fresh Rosemary:** Sprigs of fresh rosemary, roughly chopped, provide an aromatic, earthy counterpoint to the rich olive oil. Dried rosemary can be used in a pinch, but fresh is infinitely better.
*   **Coarse Sea Salt (for topping):** Flaky or coarse sea salt provides a delightful textural crunch and bursts of salinity on the surface.

## Step-by-Step Focaccia Genovese Instructions
Making Focaccia Genovese is a process that requires patience and a gentle touch, but the steps are straightforward.

1.  **Activate the Yeast:** In a large bowl, combine the lukewarm water, a pinch of sugar (if using), and the yeast. Stir gently and let it sit for 5-10 minutes until frothy, indicating the yeast is active.
2.  **Mix the Dough:** Add the flour and fine sea salt to the yeast mixture. Pour in a generous amount (about 1/4 cup) of extra virgin olive oil. Mix with a sturdy spoon or your hands until a shaggy, sticky dough forms. Avoid over-mixing; a few minutes is sufficient.
3.  **First Rise (Bulk Fermentation):** Transfer the dough to a lightly oiled bowl, turning it once to coat. Cover the bowl with plastic wrap or a damp cloth and let it rise in a warm place for 1.5 to 2 hours, or until doubled in size.
4.  **Gentle Folding (Optional but Recommended):** During the first rise, after about 30-45 minutes, perform a few "stretch and folds." With wet hands, gently grab a portion of the dough, stretch it upwards, and fold it over itself. Rotate the bowl and repeat 3-4 times. This helps develop gluten without heavy kneading. Repeat once more after another 30-45 minutes.
5.  **Prepare the Baking Sheet:** Drizzle a generous amount of olive oil (another 1/4 cup) onto a large baking sheet (approximately 13x18 inches for a thinner focaccia, or smaller for a thicker one) and spread it evenly.
6.  **Shape the Focaccia:** Gently transfer the risen dough onto the oiled baking sheet. With oiled hands, gently dimple and stretch the dough to fill the pan. Don't force it; if it springs back, let it rest for 10 minutes and then continue stretching. The goal is an even thickness.
7.  **Second Rise and Topping:** Once stretched, drizzle more olive oil over the surface (another 2-3 tablespoons). Press your fingertips deeply into the dough, creating those characteristic dimples. Scatter the fresh rosemary and coarse sea salt over the top. Cover loosely and let it rise again for 30-45 minutes.
8.  **Bake:** Preheat your oven to a high temperature, typically 425-450°F (220-230°C). Bake for 20-25 minutes, or until the focaccia is golden brown, crisp on the edges, and cooked through.
9.  **Cool and Serve:** Remove from the oven and, if desired, drizzle with a final touch of olive oil. Let it cool on a wire rack for a few minutes before slicing and serving.

## Tips for Perfect Focaccia Genovese
Achieving the quintessential Focaccia Genovese is within your reach with these expert tips:
*   **Quality Olive Oil is Non-Negotiable:** Invest in the best extra virgin olive oil you can afford. It’s a primary flavor component.
*   **High Hydration is Key:** The dough will be very wet and sticky – embrace it! This high water content contributes to the airy, open crumb. Don't be tempted to add too much flour.
*   **Gentle Handling:** Avoid aggressive kneading or punching down the dough. Treat it gently to preserve the air bubbles created during fermentation. The stretch-and-fold method is ideal.
*   **Don't Rush the Proofing:** Allow the dough ample time to rise. Proper fermentation develops flavor and texture.
*   **Hot Oven for Crispness:** A high oven temperature ensures a quick, powerful bake, resulting in a perfectly crisp crust and soft interior.
*   **Deep Dimples:** Don't be shy when dimpling the dough. These depressions are crucial for trapping oil and creating the focaccia's characteristic texture.
*   **Fresh Rosemary:** While dried herbs can sometimes substitute, fresh rosemary is highly recommended for its potent aroma and flavor.
*   **Coarse Salt:** The bursts of coarse sea salt on the surface are a signature element. Don't use fine table salt here.

## Serving Suggestions for Focaccia Genovese
Focaccia Genovese is incredibly versatile and can be enjoyed in countless ways.
*   **As an Antipasto:** Serve it as part of an Italian appetizer spread with cured meats, cheeses, and olives.
*   **With Soups and Salads:** It’s the perfect accompaniment to hearty minestrone, light tomato soup, or a fresh green salad.
*   **Sandwich Bread:** Slice it horizontally to make delicious sandwiches with prosciutto, mozzarella, roasted vegetables, or pesto.
*   **Dipping:** It’s fantastic simply torn and dipped into more high-quality extra virgin olive oil, perhaps with a splash of balsamic vinegar.
*   **Breakfast or Snack:** In Liguria, it's often enjoyed plain with a cup of coffee for breakfast, or as a mid-morning or afternoon snack.
*   **With Wine:** A glass of crisp Ligurian white wine, like Vermentino, pairs beautifully with the rich flavors of the focaccia.

## Focaccia Genovese Variations
While the classic rosemary and olive oil Focaccia Genovese is perfection, the base dough is also a fantastic canvas for other toppings.
*   **Onion Focaccia:** A very traditional Ligurian variation involves thinly sliced onions, often pre-sautéed slightly, pressed into the dimples.
*   **Cherry Tomato:** Halved cherry tomatoes, pressed into the dough before baking, add a burst of sweetness and acidity.
*   **Olives:** Sliced or whole pitted olives (like Taggiasca olives, typical of Liguria) are another popular choice.
*   **Sage:** Fresh sage leaves can be used instead of or in addition to rosemary for a different aromatic profile.
*   **Cheese:** While less traditional for the classic Genovese style, some bakeries add soft cheeses like stracchino or gorgonzola.
*   **Sweet Focaccia:** Though rare for the Genovese style, some regions make sweet focaccia with grapes or sugar and olive oil.

Mastering Focaccia Genovese is a deeply rewarding experience, connecting you directly to the culinary heart of Liguria. With its irresistible aroma, perfect texture, and simple yet profound flavors of rosemary and olive oil, this Italian baking triumph is more than just bread – it’s a taste of tradition, comfort, and pure Mediterranean joy. Give it a try, and prepare to be captivated.
